reviews.author: Rik Riel
reviews.summary: Reviewer Rik Riel suggested adding separate list heads for each migratetype in the per_cpu_pages struct to avoid skipping blocks of incorrect type, but noted that this would slow down merging of patches and is a prerequisite for his own work on 1GB allocation
reviews.raw_body:
GIven that you just skip over blocks of the wrong migratetype,
I wonder if it makes sense to have a different list head for each
migratetype in the per_cpu_pages struct.

Not that I should be saying anything that would slow down
the merging of these patches, since making the buddy allocator
more of a slow path is pretty much a prerequisite for the 1GB
allocation stuff I'm working on :)


-- 
All Rights Reversed.

reviews.author: Zi Yan
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Zi Yan suggested an alternative approach to reduce zone->lock held time, proposing that the page-allocator control (PCP) handle free page merging before acquiring the zone->lock and returning pages to the buddy system.

Reviewer Zi Yan noted that the worst-case scenario for distributed buddy allocators is when physically contiguous free pages are allocated across all CPUs, causing all CPUs to compete for a single PCP lock, and suggested a simpler approach might work instead.

Reviewer Zi Yan suggested moving the buddy allocator out of the zone->lock and into the PCP lock, allowing one PCP to steal a whole pageblock from another if it runs out of free pages.
reviews.raw_body:
IIUC, zone->lock held time is mostly spent on free page merging.
Have you tried to let PCP do the free page merging before holding
zone->lock and returning free pages to buddy? That is a much smaller
change than what you proposed. This method might not work if
physically contiguous free pages are allocated by separate CPUs,
so that PCP merging cannot be done. But this might be rare?

---

This is basically distributed buddy allocators, right? Instead of
relying on a single zone->lock, PCP locks are used. The worst case
it can face is that physically contiguous free pages are allocated
across all CPUs, so that all CPUs are competing a single PCP lock.
It seems that you have not hit this. So I wonder if what I proposed
above might work as a simpler approach. Let me know if I miss anything.

I wonder how this distributed buddy allocators would work if anyone
wants to allocate >pageblock free pages, like alloc_contig_range().
Multiple PCP locks need to be taken one by one. Maybe it is better
than taking and dropping zone->lock repeatedly. Have you benchmarked
alloc_contig_range(), like hugetlb allocation?

---

I wonder if we could go more radical by moving buddy allocator out of
zone->lock completely to PCP lock. If one PCP runs out of free pages,
it can steal another PCP's whole pageblock. I probably should do some
literature investigation on this. Some research must have been done
on this.

2026-04-03.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new page allocator called pcp buddy allocator, designed to improve scalability and reduce contention in high-thread-count and large-memory systems. The current PCP (Page Cache Pool) has limitations when dealing with concurrent allocations and frees, leading to zone->lock contention. This patch addresses these issues by allowing the PCP to grab entire pageblocks, split them outside the zone->lock, and take ownership of the block. This approach reduces lock contention, increases reuse of pages, and allows for upfront merging of buddies under the local pcp->lock. However, it also introduces concerns about fragmentation, which are mitigated by remembering partial blocks on drain and queuing their pages last on the zone freelist.
